name: Honda Prelude (BB6)
reliability: 8/10 — the H22A4 is a strong, high-revving VTEC engine that thrives on maintenance and revs, and the fifth-gen Prelude is generally durable. Two things temper the score: the Type SH's ATTS (Active Torque Transfer System) front differential is clever but complex and expensive to fix if it fails, with parts increasingly scarce, and the 4-speed automatic is weaker and less rewarding than the 5-speed manual. Clean manual cars — especially the Type SH — are quietly becoming collectible.
cost_to_own: Insurance: Moderate · Maintenance ~$450/yr · Values are rising for clean, unmodified fifth-gens (the Type SH most of all) · ATTS parts are the wildcard cost
popular_mods: Intake + header + cat-back exhaust — the H22 responds well to breathing mods
Coilovers or quality lowering springs + sway bars to sharpen the front-heavy chassis
A short shifter and fresh bushings to tighten the drive
Summer performance tires for the front-drive grip it needs
Honest take: a clean, stock Type SH is increasingly a keeper — think twice before cutting into an original one

generations.5th Gen BB6/BB8 (1997–2001): The final Prelude — sharp-edged styling, the 200 hp H22A4, and Honda's double-wishbone handling. Sold as a base/SH split in the US.
generations.Type SH (Super Handling): Added ATTS, an electronically controlled front differential that shuffles torque to the outside wheel to fight understeer and sharpen turn-in — a genuinely advanced system for a late-90s FWD coupe.
generations.Note: Honda revived the Prelude nameplate as a hybrid coupe for the mid-2020s — a very different car from this H22-powered BB6.

common_issues: ATTS failure on the Type SH — complex, expensive, and hard to source parts for
The 4-speed automatic is the weak link — the manual is both more reliable and more fun
Distributor wear/leaks, typical of Honda distributor ignitions of the era
Timing belt is an interval service — a snapped belt bends valves on this interference engine
Rust on rear arches and subframes in salt climates
maintenance_tips: Do the timing belt + water pump on schedule (~90k/7 years) — it's an interference engine
On a Type SH, service the ATTS fluid and confirm the system actually works before buying
Rebuild or replace the distributor at the first sign of a leak or misfire
Use a quality 5W-30/10W-30 and let VTEC warm up before you lean on it
Favor a manual car with records — it's the more durable and more valuable choice
